text: Upon entry, dense adhesions of the bowel to the mesh are observed, with the mesh well incorporated into the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions using a force bipolar and a non-bowel grasping instrument, carefully manipulating tissue around the bowel to apply tension and counter-tension. This process takes approximately fifteen minutes.

The surgeon assesses the mesh, determining it is well incorporated into the anterior abdominal wall and decides against explanting it to avoid potential damage. The procedure continues with retromuscular dissection.

The surgeon initiates dissection by recruiting the falciform ligament for posterior layer closure, incising the linea alba to enter the contralateral retro-rectus space. The retro-rectus dissection progresses, with the posterior sheath being grasped and the retromuscular dissection developed towards the patient's semilunar line. The retroactive space on the right side appears limited.
The surgeon begins the dissection medially in the preperitoneal fat pad, reflecting the peritoneum and peritoneal fat pad downward while the posterior sheath of the rectus is reflected upward.

The surgeon identifies transversus abdominis fibers and performs a transection of approximately one to two centimeters. The remainder of the dissection is muscle-sparing, proceeding posterior to the transversus abdominis, following the Madrid modification technique.

The surgeon dissects towards the semilunar line, incises the posterior sheath, and enters the preperitoneal space. A preperitoneal dissection is performed to create a larger space, facilitating posterior closure and mesh coverage, while preserving the transversus abdominis to minimize neurovascular injury.

The surgeon extends the preperitoneal dissection towards the retroperitoneum. The retroinguinal space is visible on the left, with trocars placed for double docking and contralateral dissection. The contralateral retrorectus space shows more sheath, eliminating the need for an extended dissection.

The surgeon begins placating the patient's abdominal wall, starting cephalad to the hernia defect. Using a number one permanent V-lock suture, the surgeon closes the ventral hernia defect by sequentially tightening the abdominal wall and applying traction. Multiple suture lines are placed under continuous tension to achieve closure without excessive force.

The surgeon re-approximates the rectus muscle caudal to the defect and closes the cephalic component, completing the reconstruction of the patient's linea alba. The mesh is then placed before closing the posterior defect, utilizing contralateral ports.
The surgeon closes the posterior sheath and visceral sac, ensuring proper re-approximation without undue tension. A 26x28 mesh is placed for broad coverage of the defect and retromuscular dissection.
